text: The surgeon begins dissection by pulling down on the median umbilical ligament, creating a pneumodissection effect. The initial incision is made horizontally into the peritoneum, avoiding downward trailing towards the lateral aspect.

The surgeon begins dissecting medially after cutting into the peritoneum, using two instruments with their tips together. The fenestrated bipolar is oriented slightly upwards to avoid bladder injury. The dissection progresses towards the symphysis pubis, then continues laterally with the same technique.

The surgeon uses the tip of the fenestrated bipolar to grasp the edge of the peritoneum against the pelvic wall, performing blunt dissection. The peritoneum is pulled inferiorly and back towards the surgeon, while carefully avoiding injury to the femoral nerve, which is pointed out during the procedure.

The surgeon meets the medial and lateral dissection planes in the middle, revealing the inferior epigastric vessels slightly to the left of the dissection area. A direct defect is visible in this region. The dissection continues inferiorly to establish the deepest margins, exposing the cord structures and vasculature to the testicle.

The surgeon exposes the symphysis pubis and dissects one to two centimeters inferior to it, ensuring the mesh will lay properly. The dissection continues across the midline to the opposite side, with the pubic tubercle visible at the midline as a landmark.
The surgeon dissects fat from the femoral canal, with the iliac and femoral vein visible as the lateral border of the canal.
The femoral vein becomes visible as the surgeon continues dissection inferior to the symphysis pubis, approaching the obturator foramen.
The surgeon inspects the lateral border of the indirect defect for the presence of a lipoma of the cord, noting minimal tissue presence in this area.

The surgeon continues the inferior dissection, peeling the peritoneum from the small indirect defect to create space for mesh placement. The chord structures are visible medially, while the vessels are seen laterally, with the edge of the peritoneum inferiorly. The goal is to clear enough room to lay a twelve by sixteen piece of mesh flat in the area.

The surgeon uses the tips of scissors to carefully dissect and pull fatty tissue medially away from the iliac vein, revealing the edge of the peritoneum. The dissection continues to create sufficient space for the mesh to lay flat in the area, with gentle traction applied to avoid damaging the vessels.

The surgeon identifies an indirect defect, a direct defect higher up medially, and a femoral defect. The iliac vein and obturator foramen are visible, along with the symphysis pubis across the midline. The surgical field is prepared for mesh placement following dissection guidelines.
The surgeon prepares a twelve by sixteen centimeter pro-grit mesh, marking its center to align with the center of the defects for optimal coverage.

The surgeon positions the marked center of the mesh near the inferior epigastric vessels, ensuring it is fully opened from medial to lateral. The mesh is laid with ample space, approximately one centimeter away from the dissected peritoneum, and positioned inferior to the symphysis pubis to prevent displacement when the bladder expands.

The surgeon completes the placement of the mesh on the inferior side and begins working on the superior side, which is less complex due to fewer anatomical concerns. The mesh is marked with a line to indicate the remaining fold, aiding visibility during minor bleeding.

The surgeon confirms that the crosshatch on the mesh is aligned at the center of the myopic tinea orifice. Using a two-oh V-lock suture on a GS-XXII needle, the surgeon demonstrates a suturing technique by threading the suture through both edges of the peritoneum, manipulating the needle with alternating hand positions, and using a fenestrated bipolar to assist in the process.

The surgeon ensures the peritoneum is completely closed with sutures to prevent small bowel obstruction. Additional sutures are placed for practice. The needle is carefully withdrawn, with the trocar opening blocked to prevent accidental ejection.
